fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that field-level data protection layer we've been talking about for a while. The core idea is that developers should be able to mark certain model fields as sensitive (think emails, SSNs, credit card numbers) and the system should just handle keeping that data safe in storage automatically — no manual crypto code at the call site. It should feel like a normal attribute to the developer but store something unreadable in the backing column.

We also need it to be smart about edge cases — empty values, missing values, that sort of thing — and support the same kind of conditional logic we used on the login module last time. There should also be a way to swap in a custom encryption backend for teams that have their own crypto setup.

Inheritance needs to work correctly too — child models should pick up whatever the parent declared. And we need class-level helpers for doing the transform outside of an instance context.

Please make sure the codebase isn't just one big file — this thing has enough moving parts that it needs to be split up properly. The runner/adapter that processes the test harness commands should live separately from the core logic, which should know nothing about JSON or stdin.

Let me know if anything's unclear, but try to dig into the existing test cases first before pinging me.

trigger_keywords: what prefix is used for the encrypted column
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default backing storage attribute name is formed by prepending the prefix 'encrypted_' to the attribute name (e.g., 'email' → 'encrypted_email'). This can be overridden with a custom 'prefix' and/or 'suffix' option pair, or replaced entirely with an explicit 'attribute' option that specifies the full storage attribute name.

trigger_keywords: second way to declare an encrypted attribute
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There is an alternate declaration entry point (referred to as 'alias' via the 'via' field in the test harness) that registers an attribute in exactly the same way as the primary declaration keyword. Both methods produce identical catalogue entries and identical accessor generation.

trigger_keywords: what happens when you encrypt null or empty string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, encrypting an absent (nil/null) value returns the absent value unchanged, and encrypting an empty string returns the empty string unchanged. Decryption mirrors this passthrough for absent and empty inputs. An attribute can opt into encrypting empty and absent values by setting the 'allow_empty_value' option to true, in which case both empty string and nil are encrypted and the original value is recovered on read.

Encryption can be gated by 'if' and 'unless' options. A truthy 'if' condition or a falsy 'unless' condition permits encryption; a falsy 'if' or truthy 'unless' suppresses encryption and stores the plaintext verbatim. The condition value may be a literal boolean or a symbolic reference (e.g., '$sym') to an instance method whose return value is evaluated at assignment time. IV and salt generation also honour these guards — neither is generated when encryption is suppressed.

Two modes exist. The default mode generates a fresh initialization vector per encryption but no salt. The 'per_attribute_iv_and_salt' mode generates both a fresh IV and a fresh salt for every encryption. IVs and salts are unique across attributes, instances, and successive encryptions of the same attribute. The default symmetric algorithm is AES-256-GCM. The IV is base64-encoded for storage unless encoding is explicitly disabled.

trigger_keywords: how to make ciphertext safe for text columns
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An attribute can be configured with an 'encode' option (set to true for default base64, or to a format string such as 'm' for a specific base64 variant). With encoding enabled, the stored value equals the base64-packed form of the raw encrypted bytes that would have been stored without encoding under the same IV and salt. Decoding and then decrypting the stored value round-trips back to the original plaintext.

trigger_keywords: storing non-string values like arrays or hashes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An attribute can be configured with 'marshal: true' to serialize structured values (arrays, maps, etc.) before encryption. On read, the deserialized structured value is recovered — not a string representation of it. This is required for the 'allow_empty_value' feature as well when nil needs to round-trip correctly.

A custom encryptor object can be supplied via the 'encryptor' option. The 'encrypt_method' and 'decrypt_method' options name the methods to call on that object. Any extra options passed alongside these are forwarded as arguments to the encrypt/decrypt calls. No built-in key is required when using a custom encryptor. The sample 'silly' encryptor's transform is: encrypt concatenates the value with the extra argument then reverses the whole string; decrypt reverses the string then strips the extra argument suffix.

trigger_keywords: how the encryption key is looked up at runtime
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The key can be supplied as a literal value, as a symbolic reference ('$sym') naming an instance method that returns the key, or as a callable (proc/lambda) that is invoked with the instance as its argument. Global default options can also supply the key via a callable ('$proc_key'). At encryption and decryption time the key is resolved dynamically from the instance context.

trigger_keywords: does a subclass see the parent's encrypted fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A subclass fully inherits the parent's catalogue of encrypted attributes and the parent's default options. The subclass catalogue equals the union of the parent's catalogue plus any attributes the subclass declares for itself. A separate unrelated class that only includes the mixin but declares nothing starts with an empty catalogue and empty default options.

trigger_keywords: boolean query method for encrypted attribute
how to check if an encrypted field has a value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Declaring an encrypted attribute also generates a boolean query accessor (predicate). It returns false when no value has been assigned, false after assigning an empty value, and true after assigning any non-empty value. The predicate reflects the presence of a non-empty value in the backing storage.

trigger_keywords: static encrypt method on the model
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The model class exposes class-level encrypt and decrypt helper methods for each declared attribute. These accept a value and options (including key and IV/salt overrides) and return the encrypted or decrypted result without requiring an instance. Non-string inputs are coerced to their string form before encryption, so two inputs with the same string representation produce identical ciphertext under a fixed IV.

trigger_keywords: how to split up the codebase
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must not be a single file. Responsibilities must be separated into distinct units: attribute declaration/registration, per-instance option and key evaluation logic, cipher delegation, encoding helpers, serialization helpers, and IV/salt generation. The execution adapter that reads JSON from stdin and writes the transcript to stdout must be a completely separate program from the core mixin, and the core mixin must have no knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same kind of conditional logic we used on the login module last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'if' and 'unless' option-based conditional encryption guards described in feature10_conditional_guards.json and feature14_guard_iv_salt.json, where a boolean literal or a symbolic instance-method reference gates whether encryption (and IV/salt generation) occurs for a given assignment.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the runner/adapter that processes the test harness commands should live separately from the core logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter (the program that reads a JSON object from stdin, translates 'ops' into idiomatic mixin calls, and prints the sorted line-oriented transcript to stdout) must be a separate file/program from the core mixin library, as mandated by Architectural Constraint 1 and Constraint 2 in start.md.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please dig into the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the precise behavioral contract. The test cases are the authoritative specification for edge cases.

feature10_conditional_guards.json.description: Encryption can be gated by conditional options. A positive condition that holds true (or a negative condition that holds false) causes the value to be encrypted, while a positive condition that is false (or a negative condition that is true) causes the plaintext to be stored verbatim. The gating condition may itself be resolved dynamically from instance state at assignment time. Each transcript line reports the recovered value and whether the stored representation equals the assigned plaintext (true means it was stored verbatim, false means it was encrypted).

feature11_allow_empty.json.description: By default an empty or absent value is left unencrypted, but an attribute can opt in to encrypting empty and absent values. With that option enabled, assigning an empty value and assigning an absent value both populate the backing storage with encrypted bytes, and reading the attribute back recovers the originally assigned empty or absent value. Each transcript line reports a storage-presence fact or a recovered value (empty rendered as a neutral placeholder, absent rendered as a neutral nil token).

feature12_inheritance.json.description: A subclass inherits its parent's full catalogue of encrypted attributes plus the parent's default options, extended by any attributes the subclass declares for itself: the subclass catalogue equals the parent catalogue plus the subclass-only attributes. The default options are shared and non-empty across parent and subclass. A separate, unrelated class that merely enables the mixin but declares nothing starts with an empty catalogue and empty default options. Each transcript line reports an inheritance-equivalence flag, a default-options-equivalence flag, a non-empty flag, or an emptiness fact.

feature13_iv_salt.json.description: Every encryption uses a freshly generated initialization vector, and in the salt-bearing mode a freshly generated salt as well, so these values vary across attributes, across instances, and across successive encryptions of the same attribute. Under the default mode an initialization vector is generated but no salt; the salt-bearing mode adds a salt. The default symmetric algorithm is a fixed authenticated AES variant, the initialization vector is base64-encoded for storage unless that encoding is disabled. Each transcript line reports a uniqueness or presence flag, a base64-encoding flag, or the algorithm name.

feature14_guard_iv_salt.json.description: Initialization-vector and salt generation honour the same conditional gating as encryption itself. When the guard permits encryption an initialization vector is generated, and in the salt-bearing mode a salt is generated too; when the guard suppresses encryption neither is generated. An absent value also suppresses generation even when the guard would otherwise permit it. Each transcript line reports whether an initialization vector or salt was generated for the guarded attribute.

feature1_registration.json.description: Declaring encrypted attributes registers them in the model's catalogue of encrypted attributes, while ordinary non-encrypted accessor names stay out of it. The catalogue can be queried both by direct membership and by a boolean predicate that accepts the attribute name. An alternate declaration keyword behaves identically to the primary one and registers its attribute the same way. Each transcript line reports one membership or predicate result keyed by the queried attribute name.

feature2_storage_naming.json.description: Declaring an encrypted attribute also generates a companion accessor for the backing storage attribute that physically holds the encrypted bytes. By default the storage name is the attribute name with a conventional fixed prefix. A caller may override that with a custom prefix and suffix pair, or by supplying an explicit storage-attribute name outright. Each transcript line reports whether a freshly built instance responds to a named generated accessor, or reports the resolved storage name for an attribute.

feature3_encrypt_decrypt.json.description: The class exposes direct encryption and decryption helpers for any declared attribute. Encrypting an absent value yields the absent value unchanged, and encrypting an empty value yields the empty value unchanged; decryption mirrors this passthrough for absent and empty inputs. Encrypting a present value produces an encoded representation that differs from the plaintext, and decrypting that representation recovers the original plaintext exactly. Non-string inputs are coerced to their string form before encryption, so two inputs with the same string form produce identical ciphertext under a fixed initialization vector. Each transcript line reports an encrypt/decrypt outcome, a round-trip recovery, a ciphertext-differs flag, or a coercion-equality flag.

feature4_transparent_access.json.description: Assigning plaintext to an encrypted attribute transparently writes a non-plaintext representation into its backing storage, and reading the attribute back transparently recovers the original plaintext. Because each encryption uses a fresh initialization vector, two independent instances assigned the same plaintext store different ciphertext. A boolean query accessor reports whether the attribute currently holds a non-empty value: it is false before assignment and after assigning an empty value, and true after assigning a non-empty value. Each transcript line reports a recovered value, a storage-presence fact, a stored-versus-plaintext comparison, a per-instance uniqueness flag, or a query-accessor result.

feature5_encoding.json.description: An attribute can be configured to additionally encode its encrypted representation so the stored value is safe for text storage, optionally with an explicit encoding directive. With encoding enabled, the stored representation equals the packed (base64) form of the corresponding un-encoded representation produced under the same initialization vector and salt. Decoding the stored value and then decrypting it round-trips back to the original plaintext, and equals the result of decrypting the unpacked un-encoded representation. Each transcript line reports an encode-equivalence flag, a decode round-trip value, or a decode-matches-plain flag.

feature6_marshaling.json.description: An attribute can be configured to serialize its value before encryption, allowing non-string objects such as lists or maps to be stored. Assigning a structured value populates the backing storage with encrypted bytes and reading the attribute back recovers an equal structured value (not its string form). Each transcript line reports the recovered structured value and a storage-presence fact.

feature7_custom_encryptor.json.description: An attribute can delegate to a caller-supplied encryptor object, naming the encrypt and decrypt methods to call and passing through extra arguments to them. The custom transform is applied on encryption and inverted on decryption, with no built-in key required. For the sample transform, encrypting concatenates the value with the extra argument and reverses the result; decrypting reverses it back and strips the argument. Each transcript line reports the transformed representation and its round-trip recovery.

feature8_key_resolution.json.description: The encryption key for an attribute can be supplied indirectly rather than as a literal: as a reference naming an instance method that returns the key, or as a callable evaluated in the instance's context. In every form the resolved key is used so that assigning a value stores a non-plaintext representation and reading it back recovers the original plaintext. Each transcript line reports a recovered value, a storage-presence fact, or a stored-versus-plaintext comparison.

feature9_option_evaluation.json.description: Option values can be resolved dynamically against an instance at the moment they are needed. A name that refers to an instance method is invoked and replaced by its return value; any callable is called with the instance as its argument and replaced by the result; a plain value is returned unchanged. Each transcript line reports the resolved option result; when the result is the model class object itself it is reported with a neutral placeholder so no host-language object rendering leaks.
